what: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R
title: NA
text: rLifting high-performance R package wavelet-based signal denoising. implements Lifting Scheme (Sweldens, 1996) zero-allocation C++ core via Rcpp, supporting offline (batch) causal (real-time) processing modes unified API.

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R
title: NA
text: wavelet packages R designed offline use: require entire signal processing. perform causal filtering (output time t depends data t), user must write sliding-window loop re-computes transform every point — O(N·W) operation. rLifting solves specialized ring-buffer architecture provides amortized O(1) per-sample processing, maintaining full compatibility standard offline denoising.

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R
title: NA
text: Offline causal denoising single package — wavelet scheme, API, different processing guarantees. Zero-allocation C++ engine via Rcpp. transforms, thresholding, reconstruction run native speed. Ring-buffer stream processor (new_wavelet_stream) real-time applications: fixed memory, constant-time updates, microsecond latency. look-ahead bias. Causal mode guarantees zero data leakage future past (verified via counterfactual leakage tests wavethresh). Adaptive thresholding based Liu et al. (2014): recursive MAD-based noise estimation three shrinkage methods (hard, soft, semisoft). Six built-wavelets: Haar, DB2, CDF 5/3, CDF 9/7 (JPEG 2000), DD4, Lazy. Plus simple API custom wavelets via lift_step() + custom_wavelet(). Diagnostic suite (diagnose_wavelet): automatic verification perfect reconstruction, vanishing moments, orthogonality, compact support, shift sensitivity.

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R
title: NA
text: Benchmarks wavethresh, adlift, nlt Doppler signal (N = 1024, Haar, 50 simulations): rLifting matches reconstruction accuracy adaptive lifting packages (adlift, nlt) running orders magnitude faster. wavethresh fast uses fixed global threshold, resulting higher MSE. causal processing, rLifting’s ring-buffer architecture ~700× faster naive sliding-window loop built wavethresh.

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R
title: NA
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("mkyou/rLifting")

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R > Quick start
title: NA
text: Process entire signal using global statistics.
code: library(rLifting)  # Generate a noisy Doppler signal x <- rLifting:::.generate_signal("doppler", n = 1024) x_noisy <- x + rnorm(1024, sd = 0.2)  scheme <- lifting_scheme("cdf97")  x_clean <- denoise_signal_offline(   x_noisy, scheme,   levels = floor(log2(length(x_noisy))),   method = "semisoft" )  plot(x_noisy, col = "grey", type = "l", main = "Offline denoising") lines(x_clean, col = "blue", lwd = 2) lines(x, col = "black", lty = 2)

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R > Quick start
title: NA
text: Process signal without using future data. Useful financial backtesting, real-time control, streaming applications.
code: x_causal <- denoise_signal_causal(   x_noisy, scheme,   window_size = 256,   levels = floor(log2(256)),   method = "semisoft" )

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R > Quick start
title: NA
text: Feed one sample time get denoised estimate immediately.
code: processor <- new_wavelet_stream(   scheme, window_size = 256,   levels = floor(log2(256)) )  stream_output <- numeric(length(x_noisy)) for (i in seq_along(x_noisy)) {   stream_output[i] <- processor(x_noisy[i]) }

previous_headings: rLifting: high-performance wavelet lifting for R > Quick start
title: NA
text: Define wavelets specifying predict update steps:
code: p <- lift_step("predict", coeffs = c(0.5, 0.5), start_idx = 0) u <- lift_step("update", coeffs = c(0.25, 0.25), start_idx = -1) my_wavelet <- custom_wavelet("MyCDF53", list(p, u), c(sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2)))  # Works in any function result <- denoise_signal_offline(x_noisy, my_wavelet, levels = 5)

text: following features planned future versions rLifting: Wavelet packets: full support Wavelet Packet Decomposition (WPD) finer frequency resolution. Irregular grids: native handling non-equispaced data (Second Generation Wavelets) without imputation. Multivariate denoising: joint denoising correlated signals.

what: 1. Speed and accuracy: optimized vs. naive
title: 3. Causal decomposition: the rLifting advantage
text: compare rLifting::denoise_signal_causal naive sliding-window implementation using wavethresh. methods process HeaviSine signal (n=500n = 500, σ=0.3\sigma = 0.3) using Haar wavelet. Causal denoising: speed reconstruction accuracy. rLifting approximately 215× faster naive loop. MSE also competitive, demonstrating ring-buffer architecture sacrifice accuracy speed.
code: library(rLifting)  if (!requireNamespace("knitr", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)   message("Required package 'knitr' is missing. Vignette code will not run.") } else {   library(knitr) }  data("benchmark_causal", package = "rLifting")  df_causal = data.frame(   Method = c("rLifting (causal)", "Naive loop (wavethresh)"),   Time_ms = c(benchmark_causal$rLifting_Time_Avg * 1000,               benchmark_causal$Wavethresh_Naive_Time * 1000),   MSE = c(benchmark_causal$rLifting_MSE,           benchmark_causal$Wavethresh_Naive_MSE) )  kable(df_causal,       col.names = c("Method", "Time (ms)", "MSE"),       digits = 4,       caption = "Causal denoising: speed and reconstruction accuracy.")

title: 3. Causal decomposition: the rLifting advantage
text: causal filter guarantees output time tt depends data including time tt. offline processing, transform operates entire signal , future event (sudden step) can alter denoised output earlier time indices wavelet coefficients span event boundary. called data leakage, look-ahead bias.

title: 3. Causal decomposition: the rLifting advantage
text: quantify leakage, use counterfactual test. generate two signals share identical noise t<128t < 128 differ : Signal : noise (event) Signal B: noise, step amplitude 5 added t≥128t \geq 128 run filter signals measure total squared difference output step (t<128t < 128): Leakage=∑t=1127[f̂B(t)−f̂(t)]2\text{Leakage} = \sum_{t=1}^{127} \bigl[\hat{f}_B(t) - \hat{f}_A(t)\bigr]^2 truly causal filter produces identical output signals [1,127][1, 127], leakage exactly zero. offline filter may produce different output step’s wavelet coefficients, support spanning boundary, influence reconstruction earlier indices. make effect visible, use longer-support wavelets (CDF 9/7 rLifting, Daubechies-8 wavethresh) floor(log2(N)) decomposition levels. Longer filters wider support, coefficients near step boundary reach past. Counterfactual leakage: lower better. Zero means look-ahead bias. causal mode rLifting achieves exactly zero leakage: output t=128t = 128 identical regardless whether step follows . filter simply seen future data. offline methods show non-zero leakage. wavelet coefficients near step boundary (t≈128t \approx 128) support extends past region, large discontinuity introduced step affects reconstructed values earlier indices. inherent property offline wavelet processing non-trivial filter support — longer filter, leakage reaches. property critical financial backtesting (look-ahead bias leads unrealistically optimistic results) real-time control systems (controller must react events yet occurred).
code: data("leakage_results", package = "rLifting")  kable(leakage_results,       col.names = c("Method", "Leakage (SSE)"),       caption = "Counterfactual leakage: lower is better. Zero means no look-ahead bias.")

title: 2. Benchmark: speed and reconstruction accuracy
text: benchmarks denoise Doppler signal (n=1024n = 1024, σ=0.3\sigma = 0.3) 50 independent noise realizations. Timing covers full pipeline (forward transform, thresholding, inverse transform) measured nanosecond precision via microbenchmark. comply CRAN time limits, results pre-computed script data-raw/generate_vignette_data.R shipped package data. raw data available via data("benchmark_offline"). comparability, packages use Haar (equivalent first-order) wavelet. Note rLifting supports additional wavelet families (CDF 5/3, CDF 9/7); Haar filter used solely ensure fair comparison.
code: library(rLifting)  if (!requireNamespace("dplyr", quietly = TRUE) ||     !requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE) ||     !requireNamespace("knitr", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)   message("Required packages 'dplyr', 'ggplot2' or 'knitr' are missing. Vignette code will not run.") } else {   library(ggplot2)   library(dplyr)   library(knitr) } #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  data("benchmark_offline", package = "rLifting")

title: 2. Benchmark: speed and reconstruction accuracy
text: Execution time full denoising pipeline (1024 points, Haar). rLifting fastest package, thanks zero-allocation C++ core (via Rcpp). wavethresh, standard DWT implementation R, moderately slower still fast. adaptive lifting packages (adlift nlt) orders magnitude slower rLifting wavethresh, fit local polynomial predictors every lifting step.
code: df_speed = benchmark_offline |>   filter(!is.na(Time)) |>   group_by(Pkg) |>   summarise(     Median_ms = median(Time) * 1000,     Mean_ms   = mean(Time)   * 1000,     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   arrange(Median_ms) |>   mutate(     Paradigm = case_when(       Pkg == "wavethresh" ~ "DWT",       Pkg %in% c("adlift", "nlt") ~ "Adaptive Lifting",       TRUE ~ "Lifting"     )   ) |>   select(Pkg, Paradigm, Median_ms, Mean_ms)  kable(df_speed,       col.names = c("Package", "Paradigm", "Median (ms)", "Mean (ms)"),       digits = 2,       caption = "Execution time for the full denoising pipeline (1024 points, Haar).")

title: 2. Benchmark: speed and reconstruction accuracy
text: Speed means nothing without accuracy. compare MSE denoised signal ground truth.
code: df_mse = benchmark_offline |> filter(!is.na(MSE))  ggplot(df_mse, aes(x = reorder(Pkg, MSE), y = MSE, fill = Pkg)) +   geom_boxplot() +   labs(     title = "Reconstruction error (MSE)",     subtitle = "Doppler signal, n = 1024, \u03c3 = 0.3 (lower is better)",     x = "Package",     y = "MSE"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: 2. Benchmark: speed and reconstruction accuracy
text: lifting-based packages (adlift, nlt, rLifting) achieve lowest MSE values. coincidence: lifting scheme operates directly spatial domain can accommodate adaptive thresholding strategies difficult implement classical DWT framework. adlift achieves best MSE uses adaptive local polynomial prediction, choosing predictor best fits local neighborhood. nlt uses permutation-based ordering find optimal lifting path applies EbayesThresh coefficient shrinkage. strategies adapt wavelet basis signal, yielding excellent reconstruction smooth piecewise-smooth functions. rLifting uses fixed wavelet (Haar example) applies adaptive semisoft thresholding. combination yields MSE values competitive nlt close adlift, running orders magnitude faster. classical DWT package (wavethresh) applies fixed wavelet universal soft thresholding. fast, reconstruction accuracy lower three lifting-based approaches. summary, marginal MSE improvement adlift nlt comes steep computational cost, making impractical real-time large-scale applications. rLifting offers practical balance: near-optimal accuracy fraction computational budget.

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: rLifting ships six built-lifting schemes: constructed via lifting_scheme(): CDF 9/7 wavelet four lifting steps (two predict, two update), making significantly smoother Haar wider support. trade-smoothness locality fundamental wavelet analysis.
code: library(rLifting)  if (!requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)   message("Required package 'ggplot2' is missing. Vignette code will not run.") } else {   library(ggplot2) }  haar   = lifting_scheme("haar") cdf97  = lifting_scheme("cdf97") print(haar) #> Lifting Scheme: haar #> Steps: 2 #> Norm:  Approx=1.4142, Detail=0.7071 print(cdf97) #> Lifting Scheme: cdf97 #> Steps: 4 #> Norm:  Approx=1.1496, Detail=0.8699

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: One strengths Lifting Scheme modularity. Unlike convolution-based wavelet implementations, lifting allows construct wavelets composing simple predict (P) update (U) steps. step local operation defined : type: "predict" "update" coeffs: numeric vector filter coefficients position: "center" (symmetric), "left" (causal), "right" (anti-causal); explicit start_idx

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: CDF 5/3 (linear) wavelet uses linear interpolation prediction averaging update: d[]−=0.5⋅s[]+0.5⋅s[+1]d[] \mathbin{-}= 0.5 \cdot s[] + 0.5 \cdot s[+1] s[]+=0.25⋅d[−1]+0.25⋅d[]s[] \mathbin{+}= 0.25 \cdot d[-1] + 0.25 \cdot d[] coefficients index offsets must match package’s internal convention (Matlab-style factorization Daubechies Sweldens, 1998):
code: # Step 1: Predict (P) — coeffs = c(0.5, 0.5), starting at index 0 p_step = lift_step(   type = "predict",   coeffs = c(0.5, 0.5),   start_idx = 0 )  # Step 2: Update (U) — coeffs = c(0.25, 0.25), starting at index -1 u_step = lift_step(   type = "update",   coeffs = c(0.25, 0.25),   start_idx = -1 )  # Combine into a scheme my_linear = custom_wavelet(   name = "LinearManual",   steps = list(p_step, u_step),   norm = c(sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2))  # biorthogonal normalization )  print(my_linear) #> Lifting Scheme: LinearManual #> Steps: 2 #> Norm:  Approx=1.4142, Detail=0.7071

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: defined, custom wavelet works seamlessly every function package — lwt, ilwt, denoise_signal_offline, denoise_signal_causal, new_wavelet_stream: detail coefficients level 1 exactly zero CDF 5/3 wavelet two vanishing moments — annihilates polynomials degree 1 (.e., ramps). property directly related predict step using linear interpolation.
code: # A linear ramp: detail coefficients should be zero x = 1:16 fwd = lwt(x, my_linear, levels = floor(log2(length(x)))) #> Warning in lwt(x, my_linear, levels = floor(log2(length(x)))): Residual signal #> at level 4 has only 1.0 samples.  # Detail at level 1: all zeros for a linear signal print(round(fwd$coeffs$d1, 10)) #> [1] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000 #> [8] 0.7071068  # Perfect reconstruction rec = ilwt(fwd) all.equal(x, rec) #> [1] TRUE

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: rLifting includes full diagnostic suite verifies mathematical properties lifting scheme. particularly useful defining custom wavelets, catch errors coefficient specification. individual diagnostic tests can also called directly:
code: diag = diagnose_wavelet(   my_linear,   config = list(     is_ortho  = FALSE,   # CDF 5/3 is biorthogonal, not orthogonal     vm_degrees = c(0, 1), # test vanishing moments for degree 0 and 1     max_taps  = 5         # expected filter support width   ),   plot = FALSE  # suppress basis plot in vignette ) #>  #> === DIAGNOSIS: LINEARMANUAL === #> [PASS] Perfect Reconstruction (Stress Test) | Max Error (Global): 1.42e-14 #> [PASS] Orthogonality (Energy Conservation) | Ratio: 1.125305 (Non-Orthogonal) [Exp: FALSE] #> [PASS] Vanishing Moments (Degree 0)        | Residual Energy: 0.00e+00 (Tol: 1.00e-09) #> [PASS] Vanishing Moments (Degree 1)        | Residual Energy: 2.08e-28 (Tol: 1.00e-09) #> [PASS] Compact Support (FIR)               | Active Taps: 5 (Max Expected: 5) #> [INFO] Shift Sensitivity                   | Energy Variation (Shift 1): 50.00% # Test perfect reconstruction separately pr = validate_perfect_reconstruction(my_linear) cat("Perfect reconstruction:", pr$passed, "\n") #> Perfect reconstruction: TRUE cat("Max error:", format(pr$max_error, scientific = TRUE), "\n") #> Max error: NULL

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: package provides three thresholding operators, accelerated C++: threshold_hard(x, lambda): keeps coefficients λ\lambda, zeroes rest (“keep kill”). Best preserving peak amplitudes, can leave noise artifacts near threshold. threshold_soft(x, lambda): shrinks coefficients towards zero λ\lambda. Smoother output, introduces systematic amplitude bias. threshold_semisoft(x, lambda): hyperbolic shrinkage, compromise. |x|>λ|x| > \lambda: sign(x)x2−λ2\text{sign}(x)\sqrt{x^2 - \lambda^2}. unified wrapper threshold(x, lambda, method) dispatches appropriate function.
code: x = seq(-3, 3, length.out = 201) lambda = 1.0  df_th = data.frame(   x = rep(x, 3),   y = c(threshold(x, lambda, "hard"),         threshold(x, lambda, "soft"),         threshold(x, lambda, "semisoft")),   Method = rep(c("Hard", "Soft", "Semisoft"), each = length(x)) )  library(ggplot2) ggplot(df_th, aes(x = x, y = y, colour = Method)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +   geom_vline(xintercept = c(-lambda, lambda), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +   labs(title = "Thresholding functions",        subtitle = expression(paste(lambda, " = 1")),        x = "Input coefficient", y = "Output coefficient") +   theme_minimal() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Hard" = "#E69F00", "Soft" = "#56B4E9",                                   "Semisoft" = "#009E73"))

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: Instead requiring user specify threshold manually, rLifting can estimate adaptively wavelet coefficients. compute_adaptive_threshold function implements recursive formula Liu et al. (2014), threshold decomposition level derived noise variance estimated via median absolute deviation (MAD) finest-level detail coefficients. thresholds used internally denoise_signal_offline, denoise_signal_causal, new_wavelet_stream, controlled alpha beta parameters.
code: set.seed(42) n = 256 signal = sin(seq(0, 4*pi, length.out = n)) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.3)  sch = lifting_scheme("cdf97") decomp = lwt(signal, sch, levels = 4)  # Compute adaptive thresholds lambdas = compute_adaptive_threshold(decomp, alpha = 0.3, beta = 1.2) print(lambdas) #> --- Adaptive Thresholds (Recursive) --- #>   d1: 1.153069 #>   d2: 0.886976 #>   d3: 0.771284 #>   d4: 0.701167

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: users need fine-grained control, forward inverse transforms can called directly, enabling custom processing decomposition reconstruction:  level control useful standard denoising functions flexible enough — example, different decomposition levels require different thresholding strategies, certain frequency bands preserved entirely.
code: set.seed(42) n = 512 t_vec = seq(0, 1, length.out = n) pure = sin(2 * pi * 5 * t_vec) + 0.5 * sin(2 * pi * 20 * t_vec) noisy = pure + rnorm(n, sd = 0.4)  sch = lifting_scheme("cdf97")  # 1. Forward transform decomp = lwt(noisy, sch, levels = 5)  # 2. Compute adaptive thresholds lambdas = compute_adaptive_threshold(decomp, alpha = 0.3, beta = 1.2)  # 3. Custom thresholding: apply semisoft to detail levels, #    but leave the coarsest level (d5) untouched for (lev in 1:4) {   dname = paste0("d", lev)   decomp$coeffs[[dname]] = threshold(     decomp$coeffs[[dname]], lambdas[[dname]], method = "semisoft"   ) } # d5 is not thresholded — preserving low-frequency structure  # 4. Inverse transform reconstructed = ilwt(decomp)  # 5. Compare df_pipe = data.frame(   t = rep(t_vec, 3),   value = c(noisy, pure, reconstructed),   Signal = rep(c("Noisy", "Truth", "Filtered"), each = n) )  ggplot(df_pipe, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = Signal)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7, linewidth = 0.4) +   labs(title = "Custom denoising pipeline (CDF 9/7, 5 levels)",        x = "Time", y = "Amplitude") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Noisy" = "grey70", "Truth" = "black",                                   "Filtered" = "#D55E00")) +   theme_minimal()

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: visualize_wavelet_basis function (plot(scheme)) generates scaling function (ϕ\phi) wavelet function (ψ\psi) cascade iteration lifting steps:  helpful understanding shape support wavelet, verifying custom wavelets produce reasonable basis functions.
code: plot(lifting_scheme("cdf97"))

title: 5. Extensions and advanced usage
text: rLifting designed high-performance extensible: Built-wavelets cover common use cases (Haar CDF 9/7). Custom wavelets can defined via lift_step + custom_wavelet used every function. Diagnostic tools verify mathematical properties automatically. Three thresholding methods adaptive threshold estimation provide flexible noise removal. Low-level API (lwt/ilwt + compute_adaptive_threshold + threshold) allows fully custom pipelines. C++ backend ensures operations run native speed.

title: 1. Introduction: offline, causal and online analysis
text: utilize doppler_example dataset included package.
code: library(rLifting)  if (!requireNamespace("dplyr", quietly = TRUE) ||     !requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)   message("Required packages 'dplyr' and 'ggplot2' are missing. Vignette code will not run.") } else {   library(dplyr)   library(ggplot2) } #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  data("doppler_example", package = "rLifting")  # Visualize the noisy input ggplot(doppler_example, aes(x = index, y = noisy)) +   geom_line(color = "grey60", alpha = 0.8) +   geom_line(aes(y = original), color = "black", alpha = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Input signal (Doppler + noise)")

title: 1. Introduction: offline, causal and online analysis
text: offline mode, algorithm access entire signal (past future). allows compute global thresholding statistics perform non-causal lifting steps, resulting smoothest possible reconstruction. Use case: historical data analysis, post-processing.
code: # Define the Wavelet Scheme (Haar for this example) scheme = lifting_scheme("haar")  # Denoise using strict offline mode # levels = floor(log2(n)) ensures maximum decomposition depth offline_clean = denoise_signal_offline(   doppler_example$noisy,   scheme,   levels = floor(log2(nrow(doppler_example))),   method = "semisoft" )

title: 1. Introduction: offline, causal and online analysis
text: causal mode, process history receiving sequentially. estimate time tt depends x1,…,xtx_1, \dots, x_t. use sliding window size WW calculate local statistics (see Liu et al 2014). Use case: backtesting trading strategies, simulating real-time systems historical data.
code: # Causal Denoising with a window of 256 points causal_clean = denoise_signal_causal(   doppler_example$noisy,   scheme,   window_size = 256,   levels = floor(log2(256)),    method = "semisoft" )

title: 1. Introduction: offline, causal and online analysis
text: Online mode allows process data point--point arrives sensor API. uses C++ ring buffer maintain sliding window state efficiently (amortized constant time per update). Use case: IoT sensors, live financial feeds, monitoring systems.
code: # Initialize the Stream Processor processor = new_wavelet_stream(   scheme,   window_size = 256,   levels = floor(log2(256)),   method = "semisoft" )  # Simulate streaming (vectorized loop for demonstration) online_clean = numeric(nrow(doppler_example)) for (i in 1:nrow(doppler_example)) {   online_clean[i] = processor(doppler_example$noisy[i]) }

title: 1. Introduction: offline, causal and online analysis
text: Combining three views illustrates trade-. offline method provides better smoothing (lower variance) “sees future”. causal/online methods reactive naturally exhibit slight phase lag.
code: # Combine results df_plot = doppler_example |>   mutate(     Offline = offline_clean,     Causal = causal_clean,     Online = online_clean   )  # Plot focusing on a specific section to see the lag ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = index)) +   # Add Truth curve   geom_line(aes(y = original, color = "Truth"), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.6) +   geom_line(aes(y = Offline, color = "Offline"), alpha = 0.8) +   geom_line(aes(y = Causal, color = "Causal"), alpha = 0.8) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Truth" = "black", "Offline" = "blue", "Causal" = "red")) +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Trade-off: smoothness vs. causality") +   coord_cartesian(xlim = c(500, 1000)) # Zoom in

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: many applications, data arrives one point time: sensor readings IoT device, financial tick data, real-time audio, physiological signals wearable monitors. analyst needs denoised estimate immediately new observation, without waiting entire series collected. rLifting addresses use case new_wavelet_stream(), closure-based stream processor backed high-performance C++ ring buffer. call processor accepts single numeric value returns denoised estimate amortized O(1)O(1) time, regardless window size decomposition depth.

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: stream processor maintains fixed-size sliding window WW recent observations. new sample arrives: oldest sample evicted ring buffer new one inserted (O(1) ring buffer update). lifting transform applied current window contents. wavelet coefficients thresholded using specified method (hard, soft, semisoft) adaptive threshold controlled alpha (decay) beta (gain). inverse lifting transform reconstructs denoised signal. recent value reconstruction returned output. warm-phase (first W−1W - 1 samples), raw input returned directly since window yet full.

what: Example: denoising a noisy sine wave
title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: simulate 1000 samples sine wave additive Gaussian noise (σ=0.5\sigma = 0.5).
code: library(rLifting)  if (!requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)   message("Required package 'ggplot2' is missing. Vignette code will not run.") } else {   library(ggplot2) }  set.seed(42) n = 1000 t = seq(0, 10 * pi, length.out = n) clean = sin(t) noisy = clean + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)

previous_headings: Example: denoising a noisy sine wave
title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: key parameters:
code: stream_proc = new_wavelet_stream(   scheme      = lifting_scheme("haar"),   window_size = 128,   levels      = floor(log2(128)),   # 7 levels, full decomposition   method      = "semisoft",   alpha       = 0.5,   beta         = 1.0 )

previous_headings: Example: denoising a noisy sine wave
title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: real application, loop replaced event listener (WebSocket, MQTT, serial port, streaming API). iterate simulated vector:
code: output = numeric(n) process_time = numeric(n)  for (i in 1:n) {   start = Sys.time()   output[i] = stream_proc(noisy[i])   end = Sys.time()   process_time[i] = as.numeric(end - start) }  df = data.frame(   Time  = 1:n,   Noisy = noisy,   Filtered = output,   Truth = clean )

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: core engine written zero-allocation C++, per-sample overhead extremely low:  latency well within requirements real-time systems. reference, audio 44.1 kHz allows ~22.7 µs per sample, sensor applications operate much lower frequencies (1–1000 Hz).
code:
# Exclude the first sample (JIT / initialization overhead) latency_us = process_time[-1] * 1e6  cat(sprintf("Median latency: %.1f µs
", median(latency_us))) #> Median latency: 9.5 µs cat(sprintf("95th percentile: %.1f µs
", quantile(latency_us, 0.95))) #> 95th percentile: 10.8 µs cat(sprintf("Max latency: %.1f µs
", max(latency_us))) #> Max latency: 35.0 µs hist(latency_us,      main = "Per-sample processing latency",      xlab = expression(paste("Latency (", mu, "s)")),      col = "steelblue", border = "white", breaks = 40)

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: visualize segment warm-phase (t>200t > 200) see denoising quality steady state:  filtered signal (orange) closely tracks true sine wave (dashed black), raw noisy input (grey) fluctuates around .
code: seg = subset(df, Time > 200 & Time < 600)  ggplot(seg, aes(x = Time)) +   geom_line(aes(y = Noisy), colour = "grey75", linewidth = 0.3) +   geom_line(aes(y = Truth), colour = "black", linetype = "dashed",             linewidth = 0.6) +   geom_line(aes(y = Filtered), colour = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Real-time denoising of a noisy sine wave",        subtitle = "Haar wavelet, W = 128, semisoft thresholding",        x = "Sample index", y = "Amplitude") +   theme_minimal()

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: causal stream processor achieves meaningful noise reduction real-time, look-ahead constant memory usage.
code:
# Exclude warm-up phase steady = df$Time > 128 mse_noisy    = mean((df$Noisy[steady] - df$Truth[steady])^2) mse_filtered = mean((df$Filtered[steady] - df$Truth[steady])^2)  cat(sprintf("MSE (noisy):    %.4f
", mse_noisy)) #> MSE (noisy):    0.2485 cat(sprintf("MSE (filtered): %.4f
", mse_filtered)) #> MSE (filtered): 0.0549 cat(sprintf("Noise reduction: %.1f%%
", (1 - mse_filtered / mse_noisy) * 100)) #> Noise reduction: 77.9%

title: 4. Real-time signal smoothing
text: larger window_size captures context denoises better, introduces latency (filter output reflects state last WW samples). applications, W=64W = 64 256256 offers good balance. levels parameter controls decomposition depth. Setting floor(log2(W)) performs full decomposition. method parameter affects aggressiveness denoising: "hard" preserves peaks better can leave artifacts; "soft" smoother may -shrink; "semisoft" offers compromise. alpha beta parameters control adaptive threshold. Higher alpha makes threshold adapt faster changing noise levels. beta > 1 makes threshold conservative (less denoising); beta < 1 makes aggressive.

title: Create an individual Lifting Step — lift_step
text: type Step type: "predict" (P) "update" (U). coeffs Numeric vector containing filter coefficients. start_idx (Optional) Manual start index. provided, ignores position parameter. Use fine-grained control. position Automatic index adjustment (used start_idx NULL): "center": Centers filter (default). "left": Causal filter (looks past). "right": Anti-causal filter (looks future).

what: Create an Adaptive Wavelet Stream Processor ('C++' Core) — new_wavelet_stream
title: Create an Adaptive Wavelet Stream Processor ('C++' Core) — new_wavelet_stream
text: Generates stateful function backed high-performance 'C++' Ring Buffer engine. implements Sliding Window + Lifting Decomposition + Adaptive Thresholding constant amortized time (O(1)) per sample.

title: Create an Adaptive Wavelet Stream Processor ('C++' Core) — new_wavelet_stream
code: new_wavelet_stream(   scheme,   window_size = 256,   levels = 1,   alpha = 0.3,   beta = 1.2,   method = "semisoft",   extension = "symmetric",   update_freq = 1 )

title: Create an Adaptive Wavelet Stream Processor ('C++' Core) — new_wavelet_stream
text: scheme lifting_scheme object. window_size Sliding window size (W). Must > 8. levels Decomposition levels (default 1). alpha Threshold decay parameter (Eq 9). beta Threshold gain factor (Eq 9). method Shrinkage method: "hard", "soft", "semisoft". extension Boundary handling ('symmetric', 'periodic', 'zero'). update_freq often recompute threshold statistics (default 1).

title: Create an Adaptive Wavelet Stream Processor ('C++' Core) — new_wavelet_stream
text: closure function processor(new_sample) accepts single numeric value returns filtered value immediately.

what: rLifting: High-Performance Wavelet Lifting Transforms — rLifting
title: rLifting: High-Performance Wavelet Lifting Transforms — rLifting
text: unified framework Wavelet Transforms using Lifting Scheme. provides robust tools offline signal analysis functional data analysis (FDA), also enabling high-performance causal processing real-time applications via specialized 'C++' core.

title: rLifting: High-Performance Wavelet Lifting Transforms — rLifting
text: Maintainer: Moises da Silva moisesdff8@gmail.com

title: rLifting 0.9.0
text: Initial release package. Implements high-performance Wavelet Lifting Transforms. Unified offline (batch) denoising. Causal (real-time) filtering using ring buffer engine. Adaptive recursive thresholding. Zero-allocation ‘C++’ core.
